agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt: You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README ![Metaflow_Logo_Horizontal_FullColor_Ribbon_Dark_RGB](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/763451/89453116-96a57e00-d713-11ea-9fa6-82b29d4d6eff.png) # Metaflow [Metaflow](https://metaflow.org) is a human-centric framework designed to help scientists and engineers **build and manage real-life AI and ML systems**. Serving teams of all sizes and scale, Metaflow streamlines the entire development lifecycle—from rapid prototyping in notebooks to reliable, maintainable production deployments—enabling teams to iterate quickly and deliver robust systems efficiently. Originally developed at [Netflix](https://netflixtechblog.com/open-sourcing-metaflow-a-human-centric-framework-for-data-science-fa72e04a5d9) and now supported by [Outerbounds](https://outerbounds.com), Metaflow is designed to boost the productivity for research and engineering teams working on [a wide variety of projects](https://netflixtechblog.com/supporting-diverse-ml-systems-at-netflix-2d2e6b6d205d), from classical statistics to state-of-the-art deep learning and foundation models. By unifying code, data, and compute at every stage, Metaflow ensures seamless, end-to-end management of real-world AI and ML systems. Today, Metaflow powers thousands of AI and ML experiences across a diverse array of companies, large and small, including Amazon, Doordash, Dyson, Goldman Sachs, Ramp, and [many others](ADOPTERS.md). At Netflix alone, Metaflow supports over 3000 AI and ML projects, executes hundreds of millions of data-intensive high-performance compute jobs processing petabytes of data and manages tens of petabytes of models and artifacts for hundreds of users across its AI, ML, data science, and engineering teams. ## From prototype to production (and back) Metaflow provides a simple and friendly pythonic [API](https://docs.metaflow.org) that covers foundational needs of AI and ML systems: 1. [Rapid local prototyping](https://docs.metaflow.org/metaflow/basics), [support for notebooks](https://docs.metaflow.org/metaflow/managing-flows/notebook-runs), and built-in support for [experiment tracking, versioning](https://docs.metaflow.org/metaflow/client) and [visualization](https://docs.metaflow.org/metaflow/visualizing-results). 2. [Effortlessly scale horizontally and vertically in your cloud](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/remote-tasks/introduction), utilizing both CPUs and GPUs, with [fast data access](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/data) for running [massive embarrassingly parallel](https://docs.metaflow.org/metaflow/basics#foreach) as well as [gang-scheduled](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/remote-tasks/distributed-computing) compute workloads [reliably](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/failures) and [efficiently](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/checkpoint/introduction). 3. [Easily manage dependencies](https://docs.metaflow.org/scaling/dependencies) and [deploy with one-click](https://docs.metaflow.org/production/intro ## Your Task When you receive a PR title and body, you must: 1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve 2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions 3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified 4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR ## Available Tools - `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project - `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders ## Required Output You must produce a structured output with: - `pr_title`: PR title - `pr_body`: PR body - `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR - `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation - `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation - `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis ## Guidelines - Explore the code before drawing conclusions - Search for files with names relevant to the PR - Identify specific functions, not just generic files - Be precise with paths (relative to base_project) - For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods

output.pr_title: Fixup how commands show up as part of the Runner API
output.pr_body:
When injecting commands into the Runner API, the generated stubs were incomplete or inaccurate, causing downstream tooling and users to see partial representations of commands. This PR aims to:
- Correctly generate full command stubs when injecting commands into the Runner API
- Ensure the stub includes command name, arguments, environment, and any relevant metadata consistently
- Update tests and stubs to reflect the corrected representation
- Preserve backward compatibility where possible while clarifying the API surface for command injection

No changes to external behavior beyond the corrected Runner API representation are expected, but existing tests should be updated to reflect the new, complete command stub structure.
output.masca_optimized:
MASCA Optimized for this PR:
- PR Goal: Ensure accurate, complete command representations in Runner API stubs when commands are injected.
- Affected Area: base_project/metaflow/R.py (Runner API implementation), with impact on base_project/metaflow/__init__.py exports, stubs in base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py, and tests in base_project/test_runner/.
- Risks: Minor API surface tweaks could affect external users or integrations relying on the previous stub shape. Mitigation: maintain backward compatibility where feasible and add clear tests.
- Expected Changes: 
  - Improve R.inject_commands to build and attach a complete command stub (name, args, environment, metadata).
  - Introduce or adjust R._format_command_stub (or similar helper) to render a full, consistent representation of a command for the Runner API.
  - If Runner is exposed via __init__, align exports to reflect the corrected API surface.
  - Update stubs generator (base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py) to emit the new stub structure.
  - Update/add tests (base_project/test_runner) to validate that injected commands produce complete and correct stubs.
- Validation Plan:
  - Run existing test suite with updated tests for command stub structure.
  - Add unit tests to cover: (a) command with multiple args, (b) environment variables, (c) metadata inclusion, (d) error handling for malformed commands.
  - Ensure that serialization/deserialization of stubs remains stable for any external consumers.
- Complexity: Moderate. Primarily a refactor of stub generation logic and a small API surface alignment, with associated tests.
- Acceptance Criteria:
  - Runner API now produces complete command stubs when injecting commands.
  - No regressions in unrelated Runner functionality.
  - Tests cover new stub shape and remain green.

files_to_modify.reason: The Runner API handling and command stub generation live here; adjust to generate complete, accurate command representations when injecting commands.
If Runner API exposure is affected by the stub changes, update exports to reflect the corrected interface.
Update the stub generator to emit the new, complete command stub structure used by the Runner API.
Update/add tests to verify the new, complete command stub generation for Runner API injections.

functions_to_modify.reason: Modify to include full details of injected commands in the generated stubs (name, args, env, and metadata).
Add/adjust helper to render a complete command stub with name, args, env, and metadata for consistency across injections.
Ensure command injection validates command structure and raises informative errors for malformed inputs.

output.analysis_summary:
Summary of analysis:
- The PR centers on correcting how commands are represented when injected into the Runner API, ensuring that stubs are complete and usable by downstream consumers.
- The most relevant code likely lives in base_project/metaflow/R.py (Runner API implementation) with possible touchpoints in exports (base_project/metaflow/__init__.py) and stub generation (base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py).
- Given the available project layout, the targeted changes should focus on: (1) enhancing R.inject_commands to assemble full command stubs, (2) introducing or refining a helper like R._format_command_stub to ensure consistent stub structure, and (3) aligning exports and tests to reflect the corrected behavior.
- Risks include potential API surface changes impacting external users; mitigation includes backward-compatible adjustments and comprehensive tests.
- Next steps for reviewers: verify the actual code locations for the Runner class (likely R.py) and adjust the proposed targets accordingly if the class name differs (e.g., Runner vs. R). Ensure tests cover the complete stub shape and that stubs generator emits the updated structure.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
Fixup how commands show up as part of the Runner API

## PR Body
When injecting commands into the Runner API, the generated stubs were incomplete or inaccurate, causing downstream tooling and users to see partial representations of commands. This PR aims to:
- Correctly generate full command stubs when injecting commands into the Runner API
- Ensure the stub includes command name, arguments, environment, and any relevant metadata consistently
- Update tests and stubs to reflect the corrected representation
- Preserve backward compatibility where possible while clarifying the API surface for command injection

No changes to external behavior beyond the corrected Runner API representation are expected, but existing tests should be updated to reflect the new, complete command stub structure.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
MASCA Optimized for this PR:
- PR Goal: Ensure accurate, complete command representations in Runner API stubs when commands are injected.
- Affected Area: base_project/metaflow/R.py (Runner API implementation), with impact on base_project/metaflow/__init__.py exports, stubs in base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py, and tests in base_project/test_runner/.
- Risks: Minor API surface tweaks could affect external users or integrations relying on the previous stub shape. Mitigation: maintain backward compatibility where feasible and add clear tests.
- Expected Changes: 
  - Improve R.inject_commands to build and attach a complete command stub (name, args, environment, metadata).
  - Introduce or adjust R._format_command_stub (or similar helper) to render a full, consistent representation of a command for the Runner API.
  - If Runner is exposed via __init__, align exports to reflect the corrected API surface.
  - Update stubs generator (base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py) to emit the new stub structure.
  - Update/add tests (base_project/test_runner) to validate that injected commands produce complete and correct stubs.
- Validation Plan:
  - Run existing test suite with updated tests for command stub structure.
  - Add unit tests to cover: (a) command with multiple args, (b) environment variables, (c) metadata inclusion, (d) error handling for malformed commands.
  - Ensure that serialization/deserialization of stubs remains stable for any external consumers.
- Complexity: Moderate. Primarily a refactor of stub generation logic and a small API surface alignment, with associated tests.
- Acceptance Criteria:
  - Runner API now produces complete command stubs when injecting commands.
  - No regressions in unrelated Runner functionality.
  - Tests cover new stub shape and remain green.

## Files to Modify
- base_project/metaflow/R.py: The Runner API handling and command stub generation live here; adjust to generate complete, accurate command representations when injecting commands.
- base_project/metaflow/__init__.py: If Runner API exposure is affected by the stub changes, update exports to reflect the corrected interface.
- base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py: Update the stub generator to emit the new, complete command stub structure used by the Runner API.
- base_project/test_runner/test_runner_injection.py: Update/add tests to verify the new, complete command stub generation for Runner API injections.

## Functions to Modify
- R.inject_commands (base_project/metaflow/R.py): Modify to include full details of injected commands in the generated stubs (name, args, env, and metadata).
- R._format_command_stub (base_project/metaflow/R.py): Add/adjust helper to render a complete command stub with name, args, env, and metadata for consistency across injections.
- R._validate_injected_command (base_project/metaflow/R.py): Ensure command injection validates command structure and raises informative errors for malformed inputs.

## Analysis Summary
Summary of analysis:
- The PR centers on correcting how commands are represented when injected into the Runner API, ensuring that stubs are complete and usable by downstream consumers.
- The most relevant code likely lives in base_project/metaflow/R.py (Runner API implementation) with possible touchpoints in exports (base_project/metaflow/__init__.py) and stub generation (base_project/stubs/runner_stubs.py).
- Given the available project layout, the targeted changes should focus on: (1) enhancing R.inject_commands to assemble full command stubs, (2) introducing or refining a helper like R._format_command_stub to ensure consistent stub structure, and (3) aligning exports and tests to reflect the corrected behavior.
- Risks include potential API surface changes impacting external users; mitigation includes backward-compatible adjustments and comprehensive tests.
- Next steps for reviewers: verify the actual code locations for the Runner class (likely R.py) and adjust the proposed targets accordingly if the class name differs (e.g., Runner vs. R). Ensure tests cover the complete stub shape and that stubs generator emits the updated structure.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Add input validation for injected commands
steps.reason: Ensure the injected command structure is well-formed and provides clear error messages for malformed inputs, preventing partial or inconsistent stubs from being generated.
steps.side_effects: Tests will need to cover validation failures (malformed commands); error messages may be relied upon by external integrations; minor API surface changes in error handling may cascade to upstream call sites.

steps.reason: Centralize rendering of a complete command stub (name, args, env, metadata) to ensure consistency across all injections and to simplify maintenance.
steps.side_effects: Serialization/formatting behavior affects all generated stubs; tests must verify formatting consistency; potential internal API changes for consumers expecting the previous stub shape.

steps.operation: Refactor inject_commands to build complete stubs
steps.reason: Use the new validation and formatter to emit full command stubs when injecting into the Runner API and attach metadata consistently across commands.
steps.side_effects: Tests and stubs generator will need updates to align with the new stub structure; potential minor backward-compatibility considerations mitigated by tests and documentation; external consumers should see complete command representations.

step_plan.summary: This PR fixes the Runner API by ensuring injected commands produce complete, consistent stubs (including name, args, environment, and metadata) through validated inputs and a centralized stub formatter, with tests updated accordingly.
